annotations.label: Gaussian elimination

anchor.snippet: is an algorithm for solving systems of linear equations

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general row-reduction algorithm, but proves over a field every matrix factors as transvections·diagonal·transvections (a Gauss-pivot variant).

annotations.label: Elementary row operations

anchor.snippet: There are three types of elementary row operations: swapping two rows

annotations.note: Transvections (add-multiple-of-row) and `Matrix.updateRow_eq_transvection` are formalized, but the three elementary operations are not unified into a single definition.

annotations.label: Reduced row echelon form

anchor.snippet: a matrix can always be transformed into reduced row echelon form

annotations.note: No definition of (reduced) row echelon form was found in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: a matrix can always be transformed into reduced row echelon form

annotations.label: Uniqueness of reduced row echelon form

anchor.snippet: This final form is unique

annotations.note: Without an RREF definition in Mathlib, uniqueness is not formalized.

annotations.label: Gauss–Jordan elimination

anchor.snippet: is sometimes called Gauss–Jordan elimination

annotations.note: No Gauss–Jordan algorithm or RREF computation is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Forward elimination and back substitution

anchor.snippet: The first part (sometimes called forward elimination) reduces a given system to row echelon form

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define forward-elimination/back-substitution phases.

annotations.label: Row reduction as matrix decomposition

anchor.snippet: row reduction produces a matrix decomposition of the original matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib provides a related decomposition M = L·D·L' with L, L' products of transvections and D diagonal, rather than the precise LU/PLU statement.

annotations.label: LU decomposition via Frobenius matrices

anchor.snippet: the first part of the algorithm computes an LU decomposition

annotations.note: Searches for `LU`/`LUDecomposition` returned no LU-decomposition statement in Mathlib (LDL exists but is different).

annotations.label: Three elementary row operations

anchor.snippet: There are three types of elementary row operations which may be performed on the rows of a matrix

annotations.note: Only the add-multiple-of-row operation is packaged (as a transvection); the three are not collected as one definition.

annotations.label: Row operations preserve solution set

anchor.snippet: these operations do not change the solution set

annotations.note: No theorem about elementary row operations preserving the solution set of a linear system was found.

annotations.label: Leading entry (pivot)

anchor.snippet: the leftmost nonzero entry is called the leading entry

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of leading entry/pivot of a matrix row.

annotations.label: Row echelon form

anchor.snippet: the matrix is said to be in row echelon form

annotations.note: No row-echelon-form predicate was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matrix in row echelon form

anchor.snippet: the following matrix is in row echelon form

annotations.note: Without a row-echelon-form definition, the example is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the following matrix is in row echelon form

annotations.label: Reduced row echelon form

anchor.snippet: A matrix is said to be in reduced row echelon form

annotations.note: No RREF predicate exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Solving a system by row reduction

anchor.snippet: Suppose the goal is to find and describe the set of solutions

annotations.note: This worked numerical example is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Suppose the goal is to find and describe the set of solutions

annotations.label: Effect of row operations on determinant

anchor.snippet: how the elementary row operations change the determinant

annotations.note: Mathlib has `det_updateRow_smul`, `det_updateRow_add`, `det_updateRow_add_smul_self`, and `det_permute` covering the three operations' effects on the determinant.

annotations.label: Determinant via row echelon form

anchor.snippet: the determinant of A is the quotient by d of the product of the elements of the diagonal of B

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the determinant of a (block-)upper-triangular matrix is the product of diagonal entries, but doesn't tie this to a Gaussian-elimination reduction algorithm.

anchors.snippet: the determinant of A is the quotient by d of the product of the elements of the diagonal of B

annotations.label: Arithmetic complexity of determinant computation

anchor.snippet: this method needs only O( n 3 ) arithmetic operations

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain complexity statements about determinant computation.

annotations.label: Inverse via Gauss–Jordan elimination

anchor.section: Finding the inverse of a matrix
anchor.snippet: The matrix A is invertible if and only if it can be reduced to the identity matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib characterizes invertibility via determinant (`isUnit_iff_isUnit_det`) and provides inverse-from-left/right-inverse lemmas, but not via Gauss–Jordan reduction.

annotations.label: Computing a matrix inverse

anchor.section: Finding the inverse of a matrix
anchor.snippet: To find the inverse of this matrix, one takes the following matrix augmented by the identity

annotations.note: The worked augmented-matrix inverse example is not formalized.

anchors.section: Finding the inverse of a matrix
anchors.snippet: To find the inverse of this matrix, one takes the following matrix augmented by the identity

annotations.label: Rank and basis from echelon form

anchor.snippet: the rank of A is 5, since there are 5 nonzero rows in T

annotations.note: Without an echelon-form predicate, the rank-equals-number-of-nonzero-rows result is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the rank of A is 5, since there are 5 nonzero rows in T

annotations.label: Arithmetic complexity of Gaussian elimination

anchor.snippet: for a total of approximately 2 n 3 /3 operations

annotations.note: Mathlib has no arithmetic-complexity analysis of Gaussian elimination.

annotations.label: Bareiss algorithm bit complexity

anchor.snippet: it has a bit complexity of O( n 5 ) , and has therefore a strongly-polynomial time complexity

annotations.note: Searches for `Bareiss` returned no hits in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bareiss entries are subdeterminants

anchor.snippet: each matrix entry generated by this variant is the determinant of a submatrix of the original matrix

annotations.note: The Bareiss algorithm is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Numerical stability conditions

anchor.snippet: Gaussian elimination is numerically stable for diagonally dominant or positive-definite matrices

annotations.note: Numerical-stability results for Gaussian elimination are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gaussian elimination over any field

anchor.snippet: Gaussian elimination can be performed over any field

annotations.note: The transvection reduction to diagonal form is proved for matrices over an arbitrary field, capturing the field-generality claim.

annotations.label: Buchberger generalization

anchor.snippet: Buchberger's algorithm is a generalization of Gaussian elimination to systems of polynomial equations

annotations.note: No Buchberger algorithm or Gröbner-basis formalization was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensor rank is NP-hard

anchor.snippet: Computing the rank of a tensor of order greater than 2 is NP-hard

annotations.note: Computational-complexity statements about tensor rank are absent from Mathlib.
